title: French apple cake
subtitle: Apple-dense butter cake with a glossy top
excerpt: A light French butter cake packed with thin-sliced apples — more apple than batter. The top sets to a thin, glossy crust. Eaten warm as a dessert or at room temperature with coffee.
sourceNotes: The style of high apple-to-batter cake found in French domestic and café baking, distinct from British apple cake by its denser fruit ratio. The gâteau invisible is the extreme version; this recipe uses a more balanced formula documented in French home cookery of the twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day it is made, while still faintly warm. Keeps 2 days at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: Softening and flavouring food by covering it in sugar or liquid and leaving it to rest. For sliced apples, tossing with sugar draws out the natural juice and softens the texture so the fruit cooks through evenly inside a cake batter.

content.text: This cake has far more apple than batter. The apple slices are the structure; the batter binds and flavours them. For the inside to cook through without the outside burning, the slices must be thin — 3 to 4 mm. Any thicker and the centre stays underdone while the top browns.

attrs.prepNote: medium (about 700 g peeled weight), peeled, cored, sliced 3–4 mm thin
50 g for the apples, 100 g for the batter

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 23 cm round tin. Toss the sliced {{eating-apples}} with 50 g of the {{caster-sugar}} and leave for 15 minutes.
the slices draws out their juice and softens them so they cook through evenly in the oven.

Whisk together the remaining 100 g of {{caster-sugar}}, the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{free-range-eggs}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} until smooth. Stir in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}}. Fold in the macerated {{eating-apples}} along with all their juice.

Pour the mixture into the tin. Bake for 40–45 minutes until the top is golden and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. The surface will look almost glazed from the apple juices. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} while still warm.
